_note: ORIGINAL content. Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline informed coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: Lists / Arrays
summary: Store ordered elements in arrays and vectors.
refresher: A fixed array has a compile-time size, while `std::vector` can grow. Vector indexes start at zero, `.size()` returns the count, and `.push_back` appends.

blocks.body: A fixed array has a compile-time size, while `std::vector` can grow. Vector indexes start at zero, `.size()` returns the count, and `.push_back` appends. Store ordered elements in arrays and vectors.

A built-in array has a fixed size that is part of its declaration. `std::vector` is a container that manages storage and can grow with `push_back`. Both use zero-based positions, but vector supplies `.size()`, checked `.at()`, and useful iteration support.

blocks.caption: The vector grows to three elements, while index one selects its second value.
Ask for checked access while a boundary is uncertain.

blocks.body: Bracket access assumes the index is valid and gives undefined behavior otherwise. The `at` method checks and throws `std::out_of_range`, which makes the bad position easier to diagnose. A final design can choose either based on its validation and performance needs.

blocks.title: Follow the data
blocks.body: Unchecked bracket access past the end causes undefined behavior. Use valid index bounds, or `.at()` when you want an exception for an invalid position.

blocks.prompt: Which is the last valid index for size three?
blocks.why: Zero-based positions are zero, one, and two, so the count itself is outside the vector.
Vector provides push_back to add one element at its end.
